Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

Do NOT capture (these become persistent self-imposed constraints that bite you later when the environment changes):
  • Environment-dependent failures: missing binaries, fresh-install errors, post-migration path mismatches, 'command not found', unconfigured credentials, uninstalled packages. The user can fix these — they are not durable rules.
  • Negative claims about tools or features ('browser tools do not work', 'X tool is broken', 'cannot use Y from execute_code'). These harden into refusals the agent cites against itself for months after the actual problem was fixed.
  • Session-specific transient errors that resolved before the conversation ended. If retrying worked, the lesson is the retry pattern, not the original failure.
  • One-off task narratives. A user asking 'summarize today's market' or 'analyze this PR' is not a class of work that warrants a skill.

If a tool failed because of setup state, capture the FIX (install command, config step, env var to set) under an existing setup or troubleshooting skill — never 'this tool does not work' as a standalone constraint.

'Nothing to save.' is a real option but should NOT be the default. If the session ran smoothly with no corrections and produced no new technique, just say 'Nothing to save.' and stop. Otherwise, act.

You can only call memory and skill management tools. Other tools will be denied at runtime — do not attempt them.

messages.content: {"success": true, "name": "nextjs-deployment-debugging", "description": "Diagnose and fix Next.js deployment issues — build failures, route registration, standalone output, Docker builds, and runtime routing.", "tags": ["nextjs", "deployment", "docker", "debugging", "build", "routes", "pages-router", "app-router"], "related_skills": ["systematic-debugging", "ecommerce-codebase-audit", "kanban-orchestrator"], "content": "--- name: nextjs-deployment-debugging description: "Diagnose and fix Next.js deployment issues — build failures, route registration, standalone output, Docker builds, and runtime routing." version: 1.0.0 author: Hermes Agent license: MIT metadata: hermes: tags: [nextjs, deployment, docker, debugging, build, routes, pages-router, app-router] related_skills: [systematic-debugging, ecommerce-codebase-audit, kanban-orchestrator] --- # Next.js Deployment & Debugging ## Overview This skill covers debugging and fixing Next.js deployment issues: build failures, routes not registering, standalone output problems, Docker build issues with GitHub Packages, and hybrid App Router / Pages Router troubleshooting. ### `pages/` and `app/` Directory Conflict When switching from App Router to Pages Router, a **stale root-level `app/` directory** (not `src/app/`) will cause: ``` pages and app directories should be under the same folder ``` **Fix:** Remove the stale `app/` directory at the repo root: ```bash rm -rf app ``` Always check for both `src/app/` AND `app/` when cleaning up. ## Working Patterns for Section-Based Content Sites When building a site that serves JSON-driven sectioned content from `getServerSideProps`, use this architecture: ### File structure ``` src/pages/index.tsx ← home page (SSR via getServerSideProps) src/pages/[slug].tsx ← all other pages (SSR via getServerSideProps) src/pages/admin/content.tsx ← admin editor (static, uses client-kit) src/pages/_document.tsx ← custom HTML shell src/pages/globals.css ← tailwind-based theme (not _app.tsx styling) src/components/sections.tsx ← all visual section components src/components/Header.tsx ← navigation bar src/components/Footer.tsx ← footer src/components/content.ts ← content resolver helper ``` ### Component section mapping pattern Each section in the page config JSON (e.g. `{id:"hero",variant:"split",content:"home.hero"}`) maps to a component via a `SECTION_MAP` record: ```tsx const SECTION_MAP: Record = { 'hero': HeroSection, 'stats-counter': StatsSection, 'trust-signals': TrustSection, 'programs-comparison': ProgramsSection, 'services': ServicesSection, 'tax-savings-calculator': TaxCalculatorSection, 'why-destination': WhyCountrySection, 'features': FeaturesSection, 'process-timeline': ProcessSection, 'testimonials': TestimonialsSection, 'cta-banner': CtaBanner, } ``` ### Data flow: pageContent shape MUST match component keys **CRITICAL:** The `pageContent` object passed to each component must have keys matching what the component looks up. The standard pattern: ```tsx // In the dispatch (pages/[slug].tsx or pages/index.tsx): const sectionData = resolveContent(content, section.content || section.id) const pc: any = {} const keyName = section.content?.split('.').pop() || section.id // "home.hero" → "hero" if (section.content || section.id) pc[keyName] = sectionData || content return ``` This creates `{hero: {...}}` from section.content=`"home.hero"`, matching what `HeroSection` expects (`pageContent.hero`). **Common failure modes:** 1. **section.id used as key instead of section.content suffix:** If the dispatch uses `pc[section.id]` (e.g. `pc["programs-comparison"]`) but the component looks for `pageContent.programs`, the data is stored under a key the component can't find. Always extract the last segment of the `section.content` dotpath. 2. **pageContent is the raw sectionData, not wrapped:** If you pass `pageContent = sectionData` directly (the resolved object), then `HeroSection` does `pageContent.hero` which is `undefined` because `pageContent` IS the hero object. The old `buildPageContent()` pattern wrapped it as `{hero: {...}}`. 3. **Components reading `pageContent` directly instead of via `.KEY`:** New components (in sections-extra.tsx) use `const d = data || pageContent || {}` where `data` is the raw section data passed as a separate prop. This is the preferred pattern. Older components (in sections.tsx) use `const c = pageContent.KEY || {}` which relies on the wrapping. Both work as long as the dispatch creates the correct shape. ### Brand tokens (CSS custom properties) Always apply brand colors as inline styles or CSS variables — Tailwind `@theme` directives in `globals.css` are ignored by Pages Router: ```css /* src/pages/globals.css */ @import "tailwindcss"; @theme { --color-primary: #1B2A4A; --color-secondary: #C9A96E; --color-accent: #C9A96E; --color-background: #FFFFFF; --color-surface: #FFFFFF; --color-surface-alt: #F5F5F0; --color-text: #1B2A4A; --color-text-muted: #5C6B7A; --color-border: #E0E0E0; --color-success: #10b981; } ``` Then in components, reference inline: `#1B2A4A` for navy, `#C9A96E` for gold, `#F5F5F0` for light backgrounds. ### SEO per page For pages, load SEO data from `content.es.json` at the matching page key path: ```tsx const seo = resolveContent(content, `${pageId}Page.seo`) || resolveContent(content, `${pageId}.seo`) || pageConfig?.seoTitle || {} ``` Set `` and `<meta name="description">` from this data. ### Content shape reference for section components The `content/es.json` in this project has this nesting: ```json { "siteName": "...", "tagline": "...", "navigation": { "navItems": [...], "ctaText": "...", "ctaHref": "..." }, "home": { "hero": { "headline": "...", "subheadline": "...", "ctaPrimaryText": "...", "ctaPrimaryHref": "...", "ctaSecondaryText": "...", "trustBadges": [...] }, "stats": { "items": [{ "value": "...", "label": "...", "icon": "..." }] }, "trust": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "items": [{ "icon": "...", "title": "...", "description": "...", "image": "..." }] }, "programs": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "tiers": [{ "id": "...", "name": "...", "price": "...", "included": [...], "highlighted": bool }] }, "services": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "groups": [{ "title": "...", "items": [{ "title": "...", "description": "..." }] }] }, "whyCountry": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "pillars": [{ "title": "...", "description": "...", "bullets": [...] }] }, "process": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "steps": [{ "number": int, "title": "...", "description": "...", "duration": "..." }] }, "testimonials": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "items": [{ "author": "...", "role": "...", "quote": "...", "rating": int }] }, "finalCta": { "title": "...", "subtitle": "...", "buttonText": "...", "buttonHref": "..." }, "taxCalculator": { "title": "...", "subtitle": "..." }, "beneluxDesk": { "title": "...", "subtitle": "...", "items": [{ "title": "...", "description": "..." }] } }, "footer": { "businessName": "...", "columns": [{ "title": "...", "links": [{ "label": "...", "href": "..." }] }], "copyright": "..." }, "whatsapp": "595...", "contactPage": { "hero": {...}, "contact": {...} }, "faqPage": { "full": { "items": [{ "q": "...", "a": "..." }] } }, "aboutPage": { "hero": {...}, "story": { "paragraphs": [...] }, "team": { "members": [{ "name": "...", "role": "..." }] }, "gallery": { "images": [{"$img":"..."}] } }, // ... 20+ more page content entries } ``` ### Brand tokens (from tokens.json) ```json { "primary": "#1B2A4A", // navy — headers, backgrounds, buttons "secondary": "#C9A96E", // gold — CTAs, accents, highlights "accent": "#C9A96E", "background": "#FFFFFF", "surface": "#FFFFFF", "surfaceLight": "#EDE8DB", // caramel tint "text": "#1B2A4A", "textLight": "#333333", "textMuted": "#6B6B6B" } ``` ## Turbopack + file: Dependencies: Symlink Resolution Failure When a `package.json` uses `file:` links for local packages (e.g. `"@ai-whisperers/sections": "file:../base/packages/sections"`), npm install creates a **symlink** in `node_modules/`. Turbopack (Next.js 16+) does NOT follow these symlinks — the build fails with: ``` Module not found: Can't resolve '@ai-whisperers/sections' ``` This happens even with `transpilePackages: ['@ai-whisperers/sections']` at top-level in `next.config.js`. **Root cause:** Turbopack uses its own module resolution that doesn't traverse `file:` symlinks. It's a confirmed behavior, not a config fix. **Three workarounds:** ### Workaround A: Copy script (recommended for local dev) **CRITICAL: When the site repo has a `packages/@scope/sections/src/` directory AND `node_modules/@scope/sections/dist/`, Turbopack resolves from `src/` not `dist/` — even when `package.json` exports point to `./dist/index.js`.** To fully resolve, copy BOTH source and compiled output before the Docker build: ```bash cp -r ../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections/src/* packages/@ai-whisperers/sections/src/ cp -r ../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections/dist/* packages/@ai-whisperers/sections/dist/ ``` Create a script that replaces the symlink with a real copy before build: ```javascript // scripts/copy-package.cjs const fs = require('fs') const path = require('path') const pkgJson = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync('package.json', 'utf8')) const link = pkgJson.dependencies['@ai-whisperers/sections'] if (!link || !link.startsWith('file:')) process.exit(0) const targetPath = path.resolve('node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections') if (!fs.existsSync(targetPath)) process.exit(0) let stat try { stat = fs.lstatSync(targetPath) } catch { process.exit(0) } if (!stat.isSymbolicLink()) { process.exit(0) } let realPath try { realPath = fs.readlinkSync(targetPath) } catch { process.exit(0) } const absRealPath = path.resolve(path.dirname(targetPath), realPath) if (!fs.existsSync(absRealPath)) process.exit(1) fs.rmSync(targetPath, { recursive: true, force: true }) ;(function copyRecursive(src, dest) { if (!fs.existsSync(dest)) fs.mkdirSync(dest, { recursive: true }) for (const entry of fs.readdirSync(src, { withFileTypes: true })) { const s = path.join(src, entry.name), d = path.join(dest, entry.name) entry.isDirectory() ? copyRecursive(s, d) : fs.copyFileSync(s, d) } })(absRealPath, targetPath) ``` Add to package.json scripts: ```json { "scripts": { "build": "node scripts/copy-package.cjs && next build", "postinstall": "node scripts/copy-package.cjs" } } ``` Note: `postinstall` runs during `npm install` but the target symlink may not exist yet (npm creates it after the postinstall lifecycle). The script handles this gracefully with an early exit. The `build` script always works because `npm install` ran first. ### Workaround B: Pre-install + copy on host (for Docker) When the base package is also on the VPS, pre-copy it before Docker build: ```bash cp -r /root/base/packages/sections node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections # Then docker build without the file: dep ``` ### Workaround C: Avoid file: links entirely — publish to npm/GHCR The permanent fix. Publish the package to npm or GitHub Packages, reference it by version: ```json "@ai-whisperers/sections": "^0.1.0" ``` This eliminates the symlink entirely — Turbopack resolves it as a normal npm package. Requires an npm publish workflow. See `references/turbopack-file-link-symlink-resolution.md` for the full session transcript with what was tried and what worked. ## Critical Knowledge: Next.js 16 Turbopack Bug **Next.js 16.2.4 with Turbopack has a confirmed bug where root App Router pages are silently dropped from the route table.** The following patterns produce NO route output: - `src/app/page.tsx` — root page is compiled (shows 4/4 in build) but produces NO `page.js` file and is NOT in routes-manifest - `src/app/[[...slug]]/page.tsx` — catch-all routes are completely ignored by Turbopack - `src/app/index/page.tsx` — same behavior, no route registered - `src/app/home/page.tsx` — same behavior **Only these routes work in App Router mode:** - `/_not-found` (handled internally) - `/_global-error` (handled internally) - Any explicit named route like `/admin/content` (works fine) - API routes like `/api/admin/content` (work fine) **Root cause:** Turbopack in Next.js 16 silently drops root-level pages that use synchronous filesystem I/O (`readFileSync`, `readdirSync`) or async patterns — even trivial `<h1>Hello</h1>` pages. The bug is in Turbopack's route discovery, not the build output. ## Workaround: Switch to Pages Router When App Router root pages fail to register: 1. **Delete `src/app/`** entirely (App Router and Pages Router can't coexist in Next.js 16) 2. **Create `src/pages/index.tsx`** using `getServerSideProps` (Pages Router SSR): ```tsx // src/pages/index.tsx import { readFileSync } from 'fs' import path from 'path' export default function Home({ content }: any) { return <main>...</main> } export function getServerSideProps() { const data = JSON.parse(readFileSync(path.join(process.cwd(), 'content', 'es.json'), 'utf-8')) return { props: { content: data } } } ``` 3. **Move other App Router pages** to Pages Router: - `src/app/admin/content/page.tsx` → `src/pages/admin/content.tsx` (static or dynamic) - `src/app/api/admin/content/route.ts` → N/A — Pages Router uses API routes differently 4. **Convert `next.config.ts` to `next.config.js`** — Pages-only mode doesn't support .ts config files ### Build output for Pages Router (working) ``` Route (pages) ┌ ƒ / ← Dynamic SSR page ├ ○ /404 ← Static error page ├ ○ /admin/content ← Static admin page ƒ Proxy (Middleware) ← Request logging ``` ## Docker Build with GitHub Packages ### Issue: `npm ci` fails with lock file mismatch When `package-lock.json` has `@ai-whisperers/*` packages but `package.json` doesn't specify versions, `npm ci` errors: ``` Invalid: lock file's @ai-whisperers/admin@0.2.1 does not satisfy @ai-whisperers/admin@ ``` **Fix:** Use `npm install --legacy-peer-deps` instead of `npm ci` in the Dockerfile. ### Issue: npm install fails inside Docker for GitHub Packages ``` npm error 404 Not Found - GET https://registry.npmjs.org/@ai-whisperers%2fclient-kit - Not found ``` **Fix:** The Dockerfile's deps stage must COPY `.npmrc`: ```dockerfile COPY package.json package-lock.json* .npmrc ./ RUN npm install --legacy-peer-deps ``` Without `.npmrc`, the Docker build can't reach `npm.pkg.github.com`. ### Issue: Content/static files missing in Docker image Even with `COPY --from=builder` in the Dockerfile, `content/` and `nexa-pages/` directories may be missing in the runner. **The `npm ci` failure causes the builder stage to fail silently** (Docker caches the previous successful build, so `COPY` from the failed builder stage is skipped, reverting to the cached runner stage). **Fix:** Always verify content files exist in the final image: ```bash docker run --rm my-image:prod ls -la /app/content ``` ## Tests for Content/Data Loading in SSR ### Live content audit script When a site serves HTTP 200 but only shows `<h1>` with no actual sections, create a standalone audit script: ```python #!/usr/bin/env python3 import json, urllib.request # Check content resolution c = json.load(open('content/es.json')) print('Content keys:', list(c.keys())) print('Home keys:', list(c['home'].keys())) # Check page config sections p = json.load(open('nexa-pages/home.json')) for s in p['sections']: key = s.get('content') or s.get('id') sc = c.get(key) print(f' Section {s[\"id\"]}: key={key} direct={!!sc}') if not sc and key and '.' in key: sc = key.split('.').reduce(lambda o,k: o.get(k) if o else None, c) print(f' dot-path={!!sc}') # Check live site h = urllib.request.urlopen('https://example.com/').read().decode() print(f'Size: {len(h)}') print(f'Has hero: {\"headline\" in h}') ``` ### Dot-notation content resolution pattern Content in `content/es.json` is nested (e.g. `c.home.hero.headline`), but section configs use dot-notation strings like `"home.hero"`. The SSR component must resolve via path traversal: ```tsx // WRONG — returns null because "home.hero" is not a flat key let sc = content?.["home.hero"]; // RIGHT — traverses home → hero → object let sc = "home.hero".split('.').reduce((o: any, k: string) => o?.[k], content); ``` Always include this helper in Pages Router SSR components: ```tsx function resolveContent(content: any, key: string): any { if (!content || !key) return null let sc = content[key] if (!sc && key.includes('.')) { sc = key.split('.').reduce((o: any, k: string) => o?.[k], content) } return sc } ``` ### Testing HTTP from inside a container BusyBox `wget` in Alpine containers can return `Connection refused` even when the HTTP server IS listening. Use Node.js TCP socket instead: ```bash docker exec $CID node -e " const http = require('http'); http.get({hostname:'localhost',port:3000,path:'/'}, r => { let d = ''; r.on('data', c => d += c); r.on('end', () => console.log(r.statusCode, d.length, 'bytes')); }).on('error', e => console.log('ERR:', e.message)); setTimeout(() => process.exit(), 3000); " ``` ## File Corruption: `patch` Tool + read_file View Pitfall **The `patch` tool writes the raw file content from the conversation context.** If the file was previously read with `read_file` (which formats output with line-number prefixes like ` 1|{`), those prefixes ARE NOT in the file on disk — they're display formatting injected by the tool. But if you use `execute_code` with a `write_file()` call that passes the read_file output string directly, the line-number prefixes get written into the file, corrupting it. **Symptoms:** - `JSONDecodeError: Extra data: line 1 column 7 (char 6)` — the `1|` prefix makes JSON invalid - Git shows `1|{` as the first line of the file - The file appears in `git log` with `+501 -501` or similar large diffs **How it happens:** ```python # WRONG — read_file's display format includes line numbers content = read_file("images.json") # Returns {"content": " 1|{\n 2| ..."} write_file("images.json", content["content"]) # Writes the line-number prefix! ``` **Fix:** 1. Restore from git: `git checkout -- <file>` 2. Or restore from a known-good source copy 3. Re-apply edits using `patch` (which operates on the actual file on disk, not the display format) **Best practice for editing JSON files:** - Use `patch` for surgical replacements (it modifies the file on disk directly) - Use Python scripts with `json.load` / `json.dump` for bulk transformations - NEVER pipe read_file output back into write_file **Verification after editing:** ```bash python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('file.json')); print('Valid ✓')" ``` ## Standalone Repo Divergence: Content Path Fixes When a standalone site repo is forked from a multi-tenant builder (e.g. `paragu-ai-builder` → `nexa-paraguay`), the content JSON files retain multi-tenant paths that break in standalone mode. ### Paths that must be fixed | Multi-tenant path | Standalone fix | |---|---| | `/s/es/nexa-paraguay/programas` | `/programas` | | `/s/nl/nexa-paraguay/contacto` | `/contacto` | | `/s/de/nexa-paraguay/benelux` | `/benelux` | | `/sites/nexa-paraguay/images/logo.webp` | `/images/logo.webp` | ### Where these paths hide 1. **Navigation hrefs** — `navigation.navItems[].href` — every nav link 2. **CTA buttons** — `home.hero.ctaPrimaryHref`, `home.finalCta.buttonHref` 3. **Program tier CTAs** — `home.programs.tiers[].ctaHref` 4. **Footer links** — `footer.columns[].links[].href` 5. **Image manifests** — `images.json` → `basePath`, every `src` and `fallbackSrc` 6. **Testimonials** — `testimonials.json` → `image`, `videoPoster` 7. **Content file image references** — only if they use `/sites/nexa-paraguay/images/` directly ### How to search ```bash grep -rn "/s/es/nexa-paraguay/" content/ # locale paths grep -rn "/sites/nexa-paraguay/images/" content/ images.json # image paths ``` Count them: ```bash grep -c "/s/es/nexa-paraguay" content/es.json # should be 0 in standalone ``` ### Dockerfile missing runtime data files When a Next.js Pages Router site loads data from `getServerSideProps` via `readFileSync('images.json')`, the Dockerfile's runtime stage MUST include that file: ```dockerfile # WRONG — images.json not copied, runtime returns null for ALL image refs COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content # RIGHT — all runtime data files included COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/images.json ./images.json # ← REQUIRED COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/nexa-pages ./nexa-pages ``` **Symptom of missing data files:** Site loads with HTTP 200, has correct header/footer/nav, but `<img>` tags resolve to empty alt-only elements. No error in logs because `loadJSON` returns `{}` instead of throwing. **Verification:** Check inside the running container: ```bash docker exec $CONTAINER ls -la /app/images.json # If "No such file or directory", the file wasn't copied in Dockerfile ``` ### Pages Router: If all pages show HTTP 200 but section content is missing Check three things: 1. **Page config has the right `sections` array** — each section needs `id` and `content` fields referencing paths that exist in `content/es.json` 2. **SLUG_MAP has an entry** — every `nexa-pages/*.json` file needs a corresponding entry in `SLUG_MAP` in `[slug].tsx`. If the slug isn't in the map, the page returns 404. 3. **Rich fallback renderer** — the generic section renderer in `[slug].tsx` must handle more than just `headline`/`subheadline`. It should also render: - `eyebrow` — small uppercase label - `body` or `content` — plain text paragraphs split by `\n` - `data.items`, `data.groups`, `data.pillars`, `data.full.items` — arrays rendered as card grids - Items support: `title/pregunta/question/term` + `description/respuesta/answer/definition/body` `docker stack deploy` can silently keep the old container running if the image tag hasn't changed. Docker doesn't re-pull or rebuild by tag; it only creates a new task when the digest changes. **Symptom:** You pushed code, rebuilt the Docker image, redeployed, but the old content still shows. **Root cause (two variants):** 1. **Swarm service update with unchanged tag:** `docker service update --image myapp:prod myapp_web` does nothing if `myapp:prod` still points to the same digest Swarm already has. Swarm sees no meaningful change and skips the rollout. 2. **Local tag not updated:** You ran `docker build -t myapp:prod .`, but the build produced a new image ID that wasn't re-tagged to `myapp:prod`. The old `myapp:prod` digest is still what Swarm knows. **Fix — use a unique tag per build:** ```bash # Build with a digest- or timestamp-based tag IMAGE_TAG="myapp:prod-$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M)" docker build -t "$IMAGE_TAG" . docker service update --image "$IMAGE_TAG" myapp_web ``` **Alternative — retag the latest build:** ```bash docker build -t myapp:prod . LATEST_ID=$(docker images --format "{{.ID}}" myapp:prod | head -1) docker tag "$LATEST_ID" myapp:prod-latest docker service update --image myapp:prod-latest myapp_web ``` **Verification that the new image is actually running:** ```bash docker service ps myapp_web # CURRENT STATE should show "Running X seconds ago", not "Running X hours ago" docker inspect --format='{{.Image}}' $(docker ps -q -f name=myapp_web) | head -c 12 # Compare with docker images --format "{{.ID}}" myapp:prod-latest ``` **Fix: Force update the service** (works only if the tag genuinely changed) ```bash docker service update --force <service_name> ``` **Verify the new container is running:** ```bash docker service ps <service_name> # Check CURRENT STATE shows "Running X seconds ago", not "Running X hours ago" ``` **Verify content changes took effect:** ```bash curl -s https://site.com/ | grep "expected string" ``` Or check inside the container: ```bash docker exec $(docker ps --filter name=<service_name> -q) cat /app/content/es.json | head -5 ``` ## Testing Build Output ### Check which routes Next.js registered ```bash # Routes manifest (App Router) python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('.next/routes-manifest.json')); print([r['page'] for r in d.get('staticRoutes',[])])" # Prerender manifest (App Router) python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('.next/prerender-manifest.json')); print(list(d['routes'].keys()))" # Build output shows "Route (app)" or "Route (pages)" section ``` ### Check compiled page files ```bash find .next -name 'page.js' -type f | sort ``` If root pages are missing (no `page.js` for `/`), suspect the Turbopack bug. ### Test standalone server ```bash cd .next/standalone && PORT=3000 HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0 node server.js & sleep 5 && curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:3000/ ``` #### Healthcheck Pitfalls Next.js standalone startup prints "Ready in 0ms" BEFORE the HTTP server actually calls `accept()`. A healthcheck immediately after startup will cause the container to restart in a loop. **Critical: 30s `start_period` is NOT enough for real Next.js apps.** Apps with admin panels, Supabase SSR, 25+ routes, and i18n content can take **45-62 seconds** to cold-start. A 30s `start_period` causes the healthcheck to fire at T+30s (before the app is ready), fail 3 retries (30-90s each), and kill the container at ~T+120s — just as the app finally finishes starting. **Recommended configuration:** ```yaml healthcheck: test: ["CMD", "wget", "--no-verbose", "--tries=1", "--spider", "http://127.0.0.1:3000/"] start_period: 90s # ← MUST be > app startup time (45-62s measured on real projects) interval: 30s timeout: 10s retries: 3 ``` Detection: `docker service ps` shows "Starting" or "Preparing" for the service. `docker service logs` shows the app reaching "Ready in Xs" but then exit 137 (OOM) or exit 255 (healthcheck failure). The service counter shows 0/1 replicas. **Diagnostic: exit code 137 = OOM, exit code 255 = healthcheck failure** - **Exit 137** (SIGKILL): The container was killed by the kernel OOM killer. Next.js standalone with SSR, Supabase, and 25+ routes needs **at least 1GB** memory limit. 512MB causes OOM after the app starts serving traffic. Increase `deploy.resources.limits.memory` to `1GB`. - **Exit 255**: The Docker healthcheck executor ran the command but it returned non-zero. Common cause: healthcheck fired before the app was ready (start_period too short), or `wget` timed out. **Unnecessary host port mapping with overlay networks** When using Traefik on a Docker overlay network (`agent-net`, `traefik_net`), **do not publish host ports** in docker-compose.yml: ```yaml # WRONG — publishes random host port (e.g. :30016->3000) that Traefik doesn't need services: web: ports: - "3000" # RIGHT — Traefik routes via overlay network, no host port needed services: web: ports: [] ``` The `ports: - "3000"` line publishes a random port on the host interface (`ingress` mode in Swarm), which is unused because Traefik connects directly via the overlay network. Remove it to eliminate unnecessary attack surface and port conflicts. **Route selection for healthchecks:** Must ping a URL returning HTTP 200. Test locally first: ```bash docker run --rm -d -p 13000:3000 <image> && sleep 5 && curl -I http://localhost:13000/<route> ``` Common pitfalls: - Root `/` may return 404 with Next 16 Turbopack route group bug (`(store)/page.tsx`) - `/admin/content` is a good target if the admin panel loads - `/api/health` is ideal if the app exposes one - BusyBox `wget` returns `Connection refused` on Alpine even when ready BusyBox `wget` (Alpine) may get `Connection refused` even when the socket is ready. Use `node -e` to test HTTP internally instead: ```bash docker exec $CID node -e " const net = require('net'); const c = net.createConnection(3000, '127.0.0.1'); c.on('connect', () => { c.write('GET / HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n'); }); c.on('data', d => console.log(d.toString().split('\r\n')[0])); setTimeout(() => process.exit(), 5000); " ``` ## GitHub Packages + Docker Build: Auth Workaround ### Issue: Docker build can't authenticate to npm.pkg.github.com When building a Next.js site with `@ai-whisperers/*` private packages, Docker build fails at `npm install` because it lacks a `GITHUB_TOKEN`: ``` npm error 401 Unauthorized - GET https://npm.pkg.github.com/@ai-whisperers%2fseo ``` Also: even when using `--build-arg GITHUB_TOKEN`, Docker's BuildKit security scanner blocks `ENV GITHUB_TOKEN` in the Dockerfile with: ``` SecretsUsedInArgOrEnv: Do not use ARG or ENV instructions for sensitive data ``` This means standard `docker build --build-arg` + `ENV` approach won't work with modern Docker. ### Solution A: Prebuild on host, use lightweight Docker image (RECOMMENDED) The `.npmrc` file reads `$GITHUB_TOKEN` from the environment, but Docker build can't access the host's environment. Instead: 1. Run `npm install && npm run build` on the host (VPS) where `GITHUB_TOKEN` is available 2. Use a separate production Dockerfile (`Dockerfile.prod`) that ONLY copies the already-built output — no npm install needed: ```dockerfile # Dockerfile.prod — use AFTER building on the host FROM node:20-alpine AS runner WORKDIR /app ENV NODE_ENV=production NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1 PORT=3000 HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0" RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs COPY --chown=nextjs:nodejs . . USER nextjs EXPOSE 3000 HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=10s --retries=3 CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://127.0.0.1:3000/ || exit 1 CMD ["node_modules/.bin/next", "start"] ``` **Important:** This Dockerfile uses `node_modules/.bin/next start` not the `server.js` from standalone mode, because the host build produces a `.next` directory suitable for `next start`. Deploy sequence: ```bash cd /root/pitchy-website git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/main export GITHUB_TOKEN=$(gh auth token) npm ci --legacy-peer-deps npx next build docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t pitchy:prod . docker service update pitchy_web --force --image pitchy:prod ``` ### Solution B: Docker build-time secret (for CI/CD) In GitHub Actions, pass the token as a Docker build secret: ```yaml - name: Build Docker run: | docker build \ --secret id=GITHUB_TOKEN,env=GITHUB_TOKEN \ -t mysite:prod . ``` Dockerfile: ```dockerfile RUN --mount=type=secret,id=GITHUB_TOKEN \ GITHUB_TOKEN=$(cat /run/secrets/GITHUB_TOKEN) npm install --legacy-peer-deps ``` ## SvelteKit Static Site Deployment (nginx + adapter-static) When deploying a SvelteKit site with `@sveltejs/adapter-static` behind nginx/Docker, the workflow differs from Next.js. ### Dockerfile (nginx alpine) ```dockerfile FROM nginx:alpine COPY build /usr/share/nginx/html COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf ``` ### nginx.conf (SPA-friendly) ```nginx server { listen 80; server_name _; root /usr/share/nginx/html; index index.html; location / { try_files $uri $uri.html $uri/ =404; } location /_app/ { expires 1y; add_header Cache-Control "public, immutable"; } } ``` **Note:** `try_files $uri $uri.html` is critical — SvelteKit prerenders `/gallery` as `gallery.html`, so nginx must serve `$uri.html` as a fallback. ### Common Pitfall: Missing prerender entries **Symptom:** Page builds fine locally (`.svelte-kit/` has the route) but 404s in production. **Root cause 1 — prerender entries list:** The `svelte.config.js` `prerender.entries` array must explicitly list every route: ```js kit: { adapter: adapter({ pages: 'build', assets: 'build', fallback: '404.html' }), prerender: { entries: ['/', '/about', '/gallery', '/tickets', '/hotel', '/schedule', '/faq', '/rules', '/contact', '/login', '/privacy', '/dealers-den', '/robots.txt', '/sitemap.xml'], handleUnseenRoutes: 'ignore' } } ``` **Root cause 2 — missing `+page.ts`:** Even with the correct entries list, a route won't prerender without a `+page.ts` file exporting `prerender = true`: ```typescript // src/routes/gallery/+page.ts export const prerender = true; ``` **Verification:** Check `build/` directory for the page: ```bash ls build/gallery.html # must exist ``` ### Cross-Page Navigation Pattern for Multi-Page Sites For 10+ page SvelteKit static sites, ensure every page is reachable: 1. **Navbar** — list ALL pages (not just a subset). For anthro-party-argentina: Inicio, Entradas, Hotel, Cronograma, Dealers Den, Galería, Sobre APA!, FAQ, Reglamento, Contacto, Inscribite 2. **Footer** — organized columns by category (Evento, Información, Redes). Include sitemap.xml link. 3. **Cross-page links** at bottom of each page — 3 related pages per page with arrow indicators. For example: Tickets page links to Cronograma, Hotel, FAQ. Schedule links to Entradas, Dealers Den, Hotel. 4. **All images must have descriptive `alt` text** for accessibility. ### Svelte svelte.config.js ```js import adapter from '@sveltejs/adapter-static'; const config = { kit: { adapter: adapter({ pages: 'build', assets: 'build', fallback: '404.html' }), prerender: { origin: 'https://yourdomain.com', entries: ['/', ...allRoutes] } } }; ``` **Pitfall:** The `origin` in prerender config fixes the canonical URL leak (`http://sveltekit-prerender`). Without it, search engines see the build hostname. ## Docker Compose + Traefik: Label Placement Pitfall **The MOST COMMON cause of "works locally, 404 online"** after a Next.js fix is **Traefik labels not being applied to the Docker container**. ### The Bug In `docker-compose.yml`, labels under `deploy:` are **ignored by `docker compose up -d`** — they are only read by `docker stack deploy` (Swarm mode): ```yaml services: web: labels: # CORRECT — service-level labels work with both - "traefik.enable=true" - "traefik.http.routers.myapp.rule=Host(`example.com`)" deploy: labels: # WRONG — ignored by `docker compose up -d` - "traefik.enable=true" ``` ### How to verify labels are applied ```bash docker inspect $CONTAINER_ID --format '{{json .Config.Labels}}' | python3 -c ' import sys,json; l=json.load(sys.stdin) for k,v in sorted(l.items()): if "traefik" in k.lower(): print(k,"=",v) ' ``` If **only** `com.docker.compose.*` labels appear, Traefik labels weren't applied. ### The Fix 1. Move labels from `deploy:` to the service level in docker-compose.yml 2. Use `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml $STACK_NAME` instead of `docker compose up -d` 3. Verify: `docker service ls` should show the service with labels applied ### CI/CD Secrets Access: Standalone vs Reusable Workflows When using GitHub Actions reusable workflows from `Ai-Whisperers/ci-cd`, the called workflow may NOT have access to the calling repo's org-level secrets (VPS_HOST, VPS_KEY, etc.), even with `secrets: inherit`. **Symptom:** CI run shows "This run likely failed because of a workflow file issue" with 0 jobs. **Fix:** Use a self-contained deploy workflow that doesn't depend on the `ci-cd` repo: ```yaml name: Deploy on: push: branches: [main] jobs: deploy: runs-on: ubuntu-latest timeout-minutes: 20 steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-node@v4 with: node-version: '20' cache: 'npm' - name: Install dependencies run: npm ci --legacy-peer-deps - name: Build run: npm run build - name: Setup SSH run: | mkdir -p ~/.ssh echo "${{ secrets.VPS_KEY }}" > ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 ssh-keyscan -H ${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }} >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts 2>/dev/null || true - name: Deploy via SSH run: | ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new ${{ secrets.VPS_USER }}@${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }} \ "cd /root/mysite && \ git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/main && \ export GITHUB_TOKEN=\$(gh auth token) && \ npm ci --legacy-peer-deps && npx next build && \ docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t mysite:prod . && \ docker service update mysite_web --force --image mysite:prod" ``` Alternatively, deploy directly from the agent via SSH (`ssh root@VPS ...`) which is faster and doesn't depend on GitHub Actions secrets at all. ```bash # Test from inside Traefik container (on same network as service) docker exec $TRAEFIK_CONTAINER sh -c 'wget -q -O - --timeout=5 http://$SERVICE_NAME:3000/ 2>&1 | head -3' # Test via Traefik directly (bypassing Cloudflare) curl -s -o /dev/null -w 'HTTP %{http_code} size=%{size_download}' -H 'Host: example.com' http://localhost:80/ curl -s -o /dev/null -w 'HTTP %{http_code} size=%{size_download}' -H 'Host: example.com' --insecure https://localhost/ ``` ### Traffic flow diagram ``` Browser → Cloudflare (orange) → VPS:443 → Traefik → agent-net → container:3000 → Next.js ``` 404 at any point in this chain breaks the site. ### SSR Content Duplication: Empty Container Shell When investigating a suspected duplicate content section, **check whether the second "section" is actually an empty component** with no visible content rather than a true content duplicate. The CTA banner section (`cta-banner`) is the most common culprit — it renders a `style="padding:2rem 1rem;background:#F5F5F0"` container shell even when the content data resolves to `null` or empty. **Fix:** Add a null-guard in the section dispatch or the component itself: ```tsx // In the dispatch loop ([[...slug]].tsx): {sections.map((section, idx) => { const Component = SECTION_MAP[section.id] const sectionData = resolveContent(content, section.content) if (!sectionData || Object.keys(sectionData).length === 0) return null // ... render })} ``` For reference material on this debugging path, see `references/ssr-content-duplication-debugging.md`. ## PPR + 'use client' + dynamic(): Content in RSC Payload But Empty `<main>` **Symptom:** Page loads with HTTP 200. Header, footer, and nav render correctly. `<main></main>` is empty in the HTML. The full content JSON is present in `self.__next_f.push` RSC script tags (visible via curl). Content appears when JavaScript fully hydrates. **Root cause chain:** 1. The `SectionsRenderer` component is marked `'use client'` at the top of the file 2. Each section component is loaded via `next/dynamic` with `{ ssr: true }` 3. With PPR (`cacheComponents: true` in next.config.js), the route is `◐ Partial Prerender` 4. PPR builds a static HTML shell at build-time that contains an empty `<main>` container 5. The dynamic content streams via RSC payload into the script tags, then React hydrates and fills `<main>` on the client **This is NOT a bug — it's how Next.js App Router + PPR works.** The content renders correctly in the browser. But if the user checks the HTML source directly (curl, View Source), they see empty `<main>`. ### When to fix it Fix when: - Users check the page source and think the page is broken - SEO crawlers don't render JavaScript (Google usually does render JS, but no guarantee) - Slow connections where the RSC payload takes time to download and hydrate ### Fixes (in order of preference) **Option 1: Disable PPR entirely** (fastest, safest) ```js // next.config.js — remove cacheComponents: true const nextConfig = { // cacheComponents: true, ← DELETE THIS output: "standalone", } ``` Then remove any `<Suspense>` wrappers from page components: ```tsx // Before: <Suspense><SectionsRenderer ... /></Suspense> // After: <SectionsRenderer ... /> ``` All routes become `● SSG` (fully pre-rendered HTML). **Option 2: Remove 'use client' from SectionsRenderer** (more work, better SSR) Convert SectionsRenderer to a Server Component. Import section components directly instead of via `dynamic()`: ```tsx // Instead of: import dynamic from 'next/dynamic' const HeroSection = dynamic(() => import('./sections').then(m => ({ default: m.HeroSection })), { ssr: true }) // Use: import { HeroSection } from './sections' ``` **Caveat:** This breaks if the section components import `'use client'` features (hooks, browser APIs). Sections that need client interactivity must stay as client components. **Option 3: Accept the current behavior** (valid if you control the SEO narrative) Content is in the RSC payload and renders via JS hydration. Real browsers see it. The `<main>` being empty in raw HTML is an architectural choice, not a bug. ### Content Resolution for sections.tsx Components When sections use the `content: "home"` pattern in page configs: ```json // nexa-pages/home.json { "id": "hero", "content": "home" } { "id": "trust-signals", "content": "home" } ``` The `resolveContent(content, "home")` returns the `content.home` object. Components like `HeroSection` do `pageContent.hero` — they expect `pageContent` to be the `home` object so they can access sub-keys. **Fix when a section returns null despite content existing:** 1. Check what key the component accesses: `pageContent.hero`, `const d = data || pageContent`, `d.groups` 2. Ensure the `content` key resolves to the right depth: - `content: "home"` → for components that access sub-keys (`pageContent.hero`, `pageContent.trust`) - `content: "home.feedback"` → for components that use the resolved data directly (`data.title`) 3. For sections using `data || pageContent || {}` with `d.groups`, check if the data has `groups` at the expected path: ```tsx // Fix: add fallback path const groups = d.services?.groups || d.groups || [] ``` **Verification:** After any content key fix, rebuild and check: ```bash curl -sL https://site.com/es/ | grep -c "expected headline text" ``` ### Dynamic Import in Server Component Error If you import directly in a Server Component and hit `Class extends value undefined is not a constructor or null`: - The section files likely have `'use client'` at the top - `dynamic()` is the correct loading pattern for client components in Server Components - When removing PPR, keep `dynamic()` with `{ ssr: true }` — it still renders server-side HTML but handles the client boundary correctly - Only exit-popup type components need `{ ssr: false }` ### pageConfig Not Loaded for Home Page (slug = 'home' Guard Bug) **Symptom:** Home page returns HTTP 200, header/footer/nav render, but `<main>` is empty. All other pages (about, servicios, etc.) render full content correctly. **Root cause:** In `src/lib/page-data.ts`: ```typescript const pageId = slug === 'home' ? 'home' : slug if (slug !== 'home') { try { pageConfig = JSON.parse(readFileSync(...)) } catch { return null } } ``` If `slug = 'home'`, the `pageConfig` loading is entirely skipped — result is `pageConfig: null`. Sections map over `pageConfig?.sections || []` which resolves to `[].sections || []` = empty array. **Fix:** Remove the slug guard. Load pageConfig for ALL slugs including 'home': ```typescript const pageId = slug === 'home' ? 'home' : slug let pageConfigPath = slug if (slug === 'home') pageConfigPath = 'home' try { pageConfig = JSON.parse(readFileSync(join(REPO, 'nexa-pages', `${pageConfigPath}.json`), 'utf-8')) } catch {} ``` Also remove the `return null` on catch — home page shouldn't 404 on config load failure. **Detection:** ```bash python3 -c "import json; exec(open('src/lib/page-data.ts').read().split('export')[1]); d=loadPageData('es','home'); print('sections:', len(d.get('pageConfig',{}).get('sections',[])))" ``` Should show section count, not 0. **The pattern:** Any `if (slug !== 'home')` guard that skips config loading for the home page is a systematic bug. The home page IS a page in `nexa-pages/home.json` and its `sections` array must be loaded like any other page. --- ## SVG JSX Parsing: `>` Characters in `d` Path Attributes **Symptom:** Component renders as blank/empty, or large sections of JSX are silently dropped from the rendered output, causing pages to lose menus, icons, or entire sections. **Root cause:** JSX parses `>` in attribute values as a JSX tag delimiter. SVG `path` elements often have `>` characters in their `d` (data) attribute: ```jsx // Some icon SVGs have path data ending with > <svg><path d="M2 3v18l[REDACTED_PHONE]v-18H2z>"/> // ← the > breaks JSX parsing ``` When this appears inline in a JSX component, the parser sees `<path d="...>` as an unclosed tag and the rest of the file renders as raw HTML outside the component. **Fix — write `>` at the end before the tag closes:** ```jsx // WRONG — > in middle of d= is parsed as JSX tag delimiter <path d="M2 3v18l4-4>" /> // RIGHT — use separate closing tag so > is in content, not attribute <path d="M2 3v18l[REDACTED_PHONE]v-18H2z"></path> // Alternative: escape the > <path d={"M2 3v18l[REDACTED_PHONE]v-18H2z>"} /> ``` The issue also occurs with `polygon points` attributes containing `>`: ```jsx // WRONG <polygon points="0,0 100,0 50,100>"/> // RIGHT <polygon points="0,0 100,0 50,100"></polygon> ``` **Detection:** If an SVG icon or section renders empty, search for `>` inside SVG path or polygon data in the JSX file. Use regex: `grep -n '".*>"' src/components/` ## Next.js 16 + Standalone: API Routes 404 in Production **Symptom:** App Router site builds clean — API routes listed in build output (`ƒ /api/menu/week`), but `curl https://site.com/api/menu/week` returns 404 in production. Static pages (`/`, `/admin/menu`) work fine. **Root cause:** `standalone` output in Next.js 16 produces a `server.js` that does NOT properly register App Router API route handlers. The files exist in `.next/server/app/api/` but aren't attached to the HTTP server. **Diagnosis:** ```bash # Routes ARE in the build output docker exec $CONTAINER ls /app/.next/server/app/api/menu/ # catalog week ← files exist # But /api/* returns 404 curl https://site.com/api/menu/week # 404 # Pages work fine curl https://site.com/ # 200 ``` **Fix:** Use `next start` instead of `node server.js`: ```dockerfile # WRONG — standalone server.js doesn't attach API route handlers CMD ["node", "server.js"] # RIGHT — next start properly registers all routes including /api/* CMD ["node_modules/.bin/next", "start"] ``` **Container port mapping:** `next start` uses `PORT` env var (default 3000), not the host port: ```bash # Old: container exposes 3010 internally docker run -d --name app -p 3010:3010 app:latest # New: next start uses 3000 internally, map host 3010 → container 3000 docker run -d --name app -p 3010:3000 --env PORT=3000 app:latest ``` **Updated docker-compose.yml:** ```yaml services: web: image: app:latest environment: - NODE_ENV=production - PORT=3000 ports: - target: 3000 # internal: must match next start published: 3010 # external: what Traefik routes to mode: ingress command: ["node_modules/.bin/next", "start"] ``` **Important:** For App Router sites with API routes, prefer a standard `node_modules/.bin/next start` approach over standalone mode. Standalone works well for Pages Router sites serving pre-rendered HTML, but App Router API routes require the Next.js server's built-in handler registration. --- ## Next.js 16 + TypeScript: CSSProperties in Inline Style Objects **Symptom:** Build fails with `Type error: Type 'CSSProperties' is not assignable to type 'Properties<...>'` when storing style objects in a typed const. **Root cause:** Next.js 16 has stricter React.CSSProperties types. A plain `const S = { ... }` infers a type that JSX's `style` prop rejects for some properties. Functions stored in the object are also invalid. ```tsx // WRONG const S = { box: { position: "fixed", bottom: 0 }, // TS error: number not assignable tabBtn: (active: boolean) => ({ ... }) // TS error: function in plain object }; ``` **Fix 1 — Type as `Record<string, CSSProperties>` and use string values:** ```tsx import type { CSSProperties } from "react"; const S: Record<string, CSSProperties> = { box: { position: "fixed", bottom: "0", left: "0", right: "0" }, // ... }; ``` **Fix 2 — Move functions outside the typed object:** ```tsx function tabBtn(active: boolean): CSSProperties { return { backgroundColor: active ? "#2d5a3d" : "#e8f0e9", cursor: "pointer" }; } <button style={tabBtn(isActive)}> ``` **CSS values that must be strings in Next.js 16 TSX:** `inset: 0`, `bottom: 0`, `left: 0`, `right: 0`, `top: 0` — these must be `"0"` (string) not `0` (number). --- ## Next.js 16 Turbopack: `--webpack` Flag Required for webpack Config **Symptom:** Build fails immediately: ``` ERROR: This build is using Turbopack, with a `webpack` config and no `turbopop` config. ``` **Fix:** Use `--webpack` flag: ```bash npx next build --webpack ``` Or add `turbopack: {}` to silence the error while keeping webpack: ```ts const nextConfig: NextConfig = { output: "standalone", turbopack: {}, }; ``` --- ## Docker Swarm: Port Conflict on Redeploy **Symptom:** Container fails to start with `address already in use` when redeploying. **Cause:** `docker compose up -d` only stops old containers, doesn't delete them. A stopped container still holds the port. **Fix:** ```bash CN=$(docker ps --format "{{.Names}}" | grep mysite | head -1) docker stop $CN && docker rm $CN # must rm, not just stop sleep 2 docker run -d --name mysite-new -p 3010:3000 mysite:latest ``` **Docker image transfer via SSH pipe (no registry):** ```bash docker save mysite:latest | ssh root@72.61.44.159 'docker load' ``` --- ## Images Not Resolving: images.json Wrapper Structure **Root cause:** Two issues stack: 1. `loadJSON('images.json')` returns the full file: `{ tenant, basePath, images: { hero: {...}, trust: {...} } }` 2. `resolveImage(images, "@img:hero.localizedEs")` traverses `images.hero.localizedEs.src` — but the passed `images` is the **wrapper**, not the inner `images` key **Fix in page.tsx:** Pass `data.images.images` instead of `data.images`: ```tsx // Before: renders empty img tags <SectionsRenderer images={data.images} /> // After: resolves actual image paths <SectionsRenderer images={data.images.images} /> ``` Apply to ALL page.tsx files that pass images to SectionsRenderer: - `src/app/[locale]/page.tsx` (home) - `src/app/[locale]/[slug]/page.tsx` (all other pages) **Verification:** ```bash curl -sL https://site.com/es/ | grep -oP '/images/hero/[^\"'\''&]+\.webp' | sort -u # Should show resolved image paths, not empty src ``` ## Troubleshooting Checklist When a Next.js site shows 404 or doesn't serve content: 1. [ ] Check build output — are the expected routes listed? 2. [ ] Check `routes-manifest.json` — are routes registered? 3. [ ] Check `page.js` files — are they compiled? 4. [ ] Test HTTP inside container — `node -e` with raw TCP connection 5. [ ] Check `content/` and page configuration files exist in image 6.
